1:
The Chronicler turns a second time to the tribe he cares for most, and the witnesses first ask why. The listed names — Pharez, Hezron, Carmi, Hur, and Shobal — are called the sons of Judah, yet only the first was Judah’s own child. The reformed John Gill notes that the posterity here is traced solely in the line of Pharez, for he alone of the five is strictly a son; the wesleyan-arminian John Wesley says the same more plainly, that the word sons means the posterity, since only Pharez was Judah’s immediate son, and the rest are set down only to show Shobal’s descent from Judah. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses the point with his eye on the parallel: Pharez only of these five was literally a son of Judah (as 1 Chron. 2:3-4 shows), while the others represent great tribal divisions or clans, and are called sons of Judah in that wider sense. For Carmi he would read the more famous Chelubai (1 Chron. 2:9), which would yield a direct line to the fifth generation — though he grants the result is of no special value and has no bearing on the rest of the section.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke explains the repetition itself. A genealogy of this tribe was already given in the second chapter; it is brought forward again here with variations, probably because different copies lay in the public registers, and the writer, finding that this second one preserved some remarkable particulars, thought it worth inserting — and no reader, Clarke adds, will regret the insertion who weighs the matter carefully.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry supplies the reason of the honour. Ezra is most particular over Judah because it was that tribe which, with its appendages Simeon, Benjamin, and Levi, made up the kingdom of Judah — the kingdom that not only outlived the other tribes in Canaan but, by the time this was written, had returned out of captivity, while the generality of the rest were lost in the kingdom of Assyria. The witnesses converge, then, on a single sense: the doubled list is no idle repetition but the register of the surviving people, the tribe through which the promise ran to David and to Christ, traced again with fresh names because in this tribe, above all, the covenant line was preserved.
2:
With Reaiah the son of Shobal the list begins to branch, and the witnesses use the verse to knit it back to the earlier register. The reformed John Gill offers only a small identification: Reaiah is the same as the Haroeh named before, the two words being of one signification. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch take up the descent of Shobal, referring the reader to 1 Chron. 2:52, and trace the line: Reaiah begat Jahath, a name that recurs often in Levite families, though of this particular descendant nothing further is known; and Jahath’s sons Ahumai and Lahad founded the families of the Zorathites, the inhabitants of Zorah, who according to 1 Chron. 2:53 were themselves descended from the sons of Shobal. The verse, they conclude, gives fuller information about the lineage of these Zorathite families than the earlier chapter had.

The other grammatical-historical witness, Charles Ellicott, reads the verse the same way and sharpens the connection. Shobal, he recalls from 1 Chron. 2:52, is there called the father of Kirjath-jearim, and 1 Chron. 2:53 adds that the Zorathites came of the clans of Kirjath-jearim; the present verse supplements that data by putting the clans of Zorah in immediate genealogical connection with Shobal himself. The names Ahumai and Lahad, he notes, occur nowhere else in Scripture, so that the register is here our only witness to their having lived at all.

The two witnesses do not divide; both treat the verse as a hinge that fastens a small Zorathite clan to the great Shobalite house already recorded. What the earlier register had left as a bare notice — that the men of Kirjath-jearim gave rise to the Zorathites — this verse fills out by naming the intermediate generations, Reaiah and Jahath, through whom the descent ran. It is the Chronicler’s method in miniature: not fresh invention but a patient supplementing of what the older lists had only sketched, so that even a family heard of nowhere else, and a clan barely a foothold in the land, is given its link in the chain that runs back through Shobal to Judah, and kept from dropping out of the tribe’s remembered descent.
3:
The list of Etam’s houses opens on a textual snag, and the witnesses handle it with characteristic care. The clause these were of the father of Etam reads awkwardly in the Hebrew, and the reformed John Gill takes it, with the Targum, to mean the princes who dwelt in Etam — a place near Zorah, named elsewhere in the company of Bethlehem and Tekoa in the hill-country of Judah. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch are blunter: the first words, these, father of Etam, Jezreel, have as they stand no meaning, and the sense is recovered only by supplying families from the previous verse, so that we read, these are the families of Abi-Etam. The Septuagint and Vulgate instead read the sons of Etam, a reading found in several codices, while others have the sons of the father of Etam — but both, they judge, are probably mere conjectures. Etam itself, they note, is a town of the Simeonites in v.32 and in the Samson narrative, and a small town in the highlands of Judah south of Jerusalem in 2 Chron. 11:6; if a place is meant here, it is the latter.

The other grammatical-historical witness, Charles Ellicott, reaches the same result by another road. Both the reading sons of Etam and sons of the father of Etam, he says, look like evasions of a difficulty; the unusual phrase is better taken as a compact way of saying that the clans named — Jezreel, Ishma, and Idbash — were the dominant houses of Etam, on the analogy of such names as Abiezer. Etam he knows from the history of Samson (Judg. 15:8) and from 2 Chron. 11:6; Jezreel here is not Ahab’s capital but the town of Josh. 15:56, and the home of Ahinoam, David’s wife (1 Chron. 3:1). The other three names are unknown. And their sister, Hazelelponi, he takes as a sister-town rather than a woman, its curious name meaning make shadow, O thou that regardest me.

The witnesses do not quarrel; they model instead how a corrupt clause is faced honestly. None pretends the Hebrew is smooth, and none forces it; they weigh the versions, mark the likely conjectures, and settle for the most probable sense — that a group of Judahite clans is being fastened to the town of Etam. Even here, where the text is bruised, the register keeps its purpose, numbering the families that dwelt in the hill-country of Judah among the descendants of Hur.
4:
Two more fathers of towns close the little list of Hur’s descendants — Penuel the father of Gedor, and Ezer the father of Hushah. The reformed John Gill reads father here as prince or founder, and would identify Penuel with the Hareph named earlier as father of Gedor. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch fill in the geography and notice a small puzzle. Penuel is elsewhere the name of a place in the land east of Jordan (Gen. 32:31, Judg. 8:8) and once the name of a man; Gedor is the town in the mountains of Judah still traceable in the ruin Jedur (Josh. 15:58). But Penuel is here called father of Gedor, while at v.18 one Jered bears that same title — from which they infer that the inhabitants of Gedor were reckoned descendants of both. Ezer, whose name means help, is father of Hushah, itself the name of a place, the birthplace of the hero Sibbecai (1 Chron. 11:29). All those named in the two verses are then gathered up as the sons of Hur, the firstborn of Ephratah (1 Chron. 2:19), the father of Bethlehem.

Charles Ellicott, the other grammatical-historical voice, treats the closing clause as a subscription to the whole short list of verses two through four: both the Shobalite clans of Zorah and the houses just named were sons of Hur. He notes the same trans-Jordan Penuel of Judg. 8:8, but insists a Judean town or clan is meant here; that Ezer, like the others, is a name borne by clans and localities as well as by men; and that Hur, called firstborn of Ephratah, is at 1 Chron. 2:51 the father of Bethlehem — as is his son Salma in the same chapter, the two notices standing side by side.

The witnesses read the verse, then, as the quiet completion of Hur’s genealogy and its anchoring in Bethlehem. It is no small thing that these obscure founders of Gedor and Hushah are traced to the house of Ephratah and to the town of Bethlehem, for that is the town from which David would come, and from David the promised King. The register lingers over these unremembered clans precisely because they stand in the line that runs to the city of the great birth, and the witnesses, tracing father after father, keep the reader’s eye on the Bethlehem toward which the whole tribe of Judah is bending.
5:
The register turns to Ashur, the father of Tekoa, and his two wives, Helah and Naarah. The reformed John Gill sets his pedigree in place: Ashur was a son of Hezron by Abiah, the posthumous child noted in the earlier chapter. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch confirm it — according to 1 Chron. 2:24 he was born to Hezron after his father’s death — and then work carefully through the children the two wives bore. From Naarah came four sons, each the head of a family: Ahuzam, of whom nothing further is known; Hepher, likewise unknown, and to be kept distinct from the Gileadite Hepher of Num. 26:32, the conjecture that would tie his name to the land of Hepher (1 Kings 4:10) being, they judge, poorly supported; Temani, whose name, man of the south, may mark a person but is more likely that of a family; and Haahashtari, said to be descended from one Ahashtar, wholly unknown.

The witnesses do not differ; the interest of the verse for them lies in its very obscurity. Here is a man, Ashur, tied firmly to the great stock of Hezron and honoured as the father of Tekoa, yet his sons by Naarah are names and nothing more, families that rose and passed leaving no story behind. Keil and Delitzsch’s patient sifting, distinguishing this Hepher from that, refusing the unsupported guess, is itself instructive: the Chronicler’s fidelity is matched by the commentator’s, each unwilling to make more of a name than the record warrants.

So the verse stands as one of the register’s plainer stretches — a father and his two wives and a first tally of children — set down because they belonged to the house of Judah. The witnesses fix Ashur’s descent from Hezron, distinguish his obscure sons from their better-known namesakes, and number soberly the four that Naarah bore him, content to preserve a link in Judah’s line even where no story survives to adorn it. That the man is called the father of Tekoa binds these forgotten households to a settled place in the tribe; and that is enough, for the register’s purpose, to keep them among the remembered families of Judah.
6:
The verse simply enrolls the four sons Naarah bore — Ahuzam, Hepher, Temeni, and Haahashtari — and closes, these were the sons of Naarah. The witnesses have little to add and say so with candor. The reformed John Gill remarks only that of these men we have no account elsewhere; they appear here and nowhere again. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, having already named the four under the previous verse, use the notice to round off Naarah’s branch before turning to Helah’s, and observe that these names, like so many in the chapter, are met with only in this place.

Yet the witnesses find the bare enrollment worth pausing over. The register’s care to distinguish the two wives — Naarah first, then Helah — divides Ashur’s house into two co-ordinate branches, and the formula that seals each keeps them from being confused. That a woman’s sons should be counted out by name and closed with such a seal is the register doing its proper work, holding the households of Judah distinct even when nothing but the names survives. Keil and Delitzsch’s care to mark each name as otherwise unknown is not a complaint but a fidelity: they will not invent a history for men the record leaves silent, nor collapse one Hepher into another simply to fill the gap.

So the verse teaches, in its very plainness, the patience of a genealogy. Naarah’s four sons had their day, founded their families, and passed from memory, and the Chronicler preserves them not because their deeds were great but because their blood was Judah’s. The witnesses read the closing formula as a small seal of belonging: these were hers, and through her Ashur’s, and through Ashur Hezron’s, and so the line holds unbroken. It is enough, for the register’s purpose, that they be named and numbered and set in their place among the families of the tribe, the sons of Naarah kept in the book though the world has forgotten every face behind the names. Gill’s single line and Keil and Delitzsch’s brief notice together make no more of the four than the text allows, and that restraint is itself the lesson: a genealogy keeps faith with the dead by naming them truly, neither less nor more, and trusting the God who remembers to supply what the record cannot.
7:
The three sons of Helah, Ashur’s other wife, are named — Zereth, Jezoar, and Ethnan — and the witnesses attend mostly to two small matters of the text. The reformed John Gill notes only that Helah was the other wife, her sons set beside Naarah’s to complete the house of Ashur. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch fix a point of vocalization: where the written text (the Kethibh) has Jezoar, the margin (the Keri) reads Zohar — a name borne by a son of Simeon (Gen. 46:10) and by the Hittite chief with whom Abraham treated for a burying-place (Gen. 23:8) — but the son of Helah, they caution, has nothing to do with either of those men; the coincidence of a name is no proof of kinship. These sons, they add, are not met with anywhere else.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke presses a different repair. After the word Ethnan, he argues, we should with the Targum read Coz, whose posterity is set down in the very next verse; otherwise Coz appears there abruptly, a consequence without an antecedent. And this Coz, Clarke suggests, was probably the same man as Kenaz, a name that recurs further down the chapter.

The witnesses thus read the verse as a place where the received text needs watching. Keil and Delitzsch guard against reading too much into a shared name, refusing to knit the son of Helah to Simeon’s Zohar or to Abraham’s Hittite; Clarke, conversely, would knit the broken thread back together by restoring a name the copyists seem to have dropped. Neither charge is idle, and together they show the commentators’ double discipline over a genealogy — cautious where a coincidence of names might mislead, and ready to mend the list where a name has plainly fallen out. The three sons of Helah, otherwise unknown, are held in place by just this careful handling, that the register of Ashur’s house might stand whole. It is a small verse — three names and two disputed points of the text — yet in it the witnesses show how much patient care the mere transmission of a name-list demands, and how easily, without it, a genealogy might mislead or break.
8:
With Coz the register enters a passage whose place in the tribe is admittedly obscure, and the witnesses say so plainly. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch judge that verses eight through ten form a fragment whose connection with the sons of Judah in the second chapter is not clear; the name Coz occurs only here, and the sons he begets — Anub, Zobebah, and the families of Aharhel — are found nowhere else. If, they add, there is any genealogical link between the man Jabez, soon to be named, and the town of that name in 1 Chron. 2:55, then these persons would belong among the descendants of Shobal. The reformed John Gill takes Coz more simply as another son of Helah, brother of those just named, reading the fragment as a continuation rather than a puzzle.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke lodges a textual complaint that will matter for the famous verses ahead: Jabez, he says, ought to be mentioned at the end of this verse, else he stands in the next as a consequent without an antecedent — a name, that is, dropped in with nothing to hang on. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott presses the same connection from the other side. Jabez was a town of Judah (1 Chron. 2:55) inhabited by clans of scribes, of the lineage of Salma the son of Hur; this, he says, gives the clue to the passage, and seems to prove that verses eight through ten belong to the list begun at verse five, so that Coz too concerns some clans of the Hurites, even though he is not put into express genealogical connection with them. The words and Coz, he suggests, may simply have fallen out of the Hebrew, since the same phrase follows at once.

Ellicott lingers, too, over the meanings hidden in the names. Anub he compares with a town near Debir, and takes the word to mean grape-town, so that Coz begat Anub — thorn begetting grape — recalls the Lord’s question whether men gather grapes of thorns (Matt. 7:16). Zobebah he renders she that goeth softly, perhaps from a neighbouring brook; Aharhel, behind the rampart, and Harum, the elevated, perhaps a citadel that gave the clan its name. The witnesses leave the verse, then, as a bruised and disputed fragment, its links half-lost — but a fragment they will not discard, because it carries in it the seedbed of the prayer of Jabez, and behind that prayer, the scribes of a little Judahite town.
9:
Out of a run of bare names the register suddenly lifts one man into the light: Jabez was more honourable than his brethren. The witnesses gather round both his honour and the strange, sorrowful name that frames it. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry makes the eminence the heart of the verse — Jabez was qualified above his brethren by the divine grace and dignified above them by the divine providence; they did virtuously, but he excelled them all. Henry will not guess idly at the ground of that honour: it was not, he thinks, for a greater estate or a magistracy or feats of war, but most likely for his learning and his piety. For learning, because the families of the scribes are said to have dwelt at Jabez (1 Chron. 2:55), a city that probably took its name from him, and the Jews remembered him as a famous doctor of the law; for piety, because the verses show him a praying man, and the way to be truly great is to be truly good and to pray much.

On the name the witnesses read a mother’s grief written into a child. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott glosses the Hebrew: the word rendered sorrow is rather pain, and the naming recalls the mothers of Genesis who stamped their travail on a son — Rachel above all, who with her dying breath called her child Benoni (Gen. 35:18). He notes too that the very form of the phrase, more honourable than his brethren, ties Jabez back to the sons of Coz in v.8, so that his brethren are likely those unnamed kinsmen. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley draws the double lesson the mother meant: the sorrowful name was a memorandum to herself, to be thankful ever after to the God who brought her through that pain, and a memorandum to him, that she had borne him into a vale of tears where he must look for few days and full of trouble — and the very sorrow in his name might put a seriousness upon his spirit.

The reformed John Gill weighs the man’s identity and the traditions about him. He was more honourable, Gill allows — wiser in the law than his brethren, as the Targum adds, or a man of wealth, or of strength and courage, or, most likely, a man of devotion and religion, a man of God. Who exactly he was is not easy to say; probably he belonged to one of the families of Aharhel just named. But Gill firmly rejects the old rabbinic guess, which the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch record only to set aside, that Jabez was Othniel — for Othniel is spoken of later in the chapter as a distinct man (v.13), and of Jabez himself, the grammatical-historical witnesses concede, we know nothing beyond what these two verses tell. Honour, learning, piety, and a name of pain: on these the witnesses agree, and they leave the reader leaning toward the prayer that the next verse will record.
10:
The one deed the register keeps of Jabez is a prayer, and the witnesses treat it as a pattern of what prayer should be. Jabez called on the God of Israel — and the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lingers first on the One addressed: not any god of the nations, but the living and true God, the God in covenant with his people, the God with whom Jacob wrestled and prevailed. As Solomon prayed for wisdom at his setting out, so Jabez, it seems, set himself at the threshold of life to acknowledge God in all his ways. Henry then unfolds the four petitions. First, that God would bless him indeed — blessing, he would bless him, with manifold and abundant and above all spiritual blessings, perhaps echoing the promise to Abraham, in blessing I will bless thee (Gen. 22:17). Second, that he would enlarge his coast, prospering his endeavours; and as Jabez sought a larger portion, so we may pray God to enlarge our hearts and our portion in the heavenly Canaan. Third, that God’s hand might be with him — for Moses had prayed that Judah’s own hands might be sufficient for him (Deut. 33:7), but Jabez knows no hand suffices unless God’s hand go with it. Fourth, that he would keep him from evil, that it might not grieve him — the evil of sin and of trouble both — with a plain allusion, in the Hebrew, to his own name of pain.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the grammar as a suppressed vow. The petitions run as a condition — if indeed thou wilt bless me… and if thine hand will be with me — with the consequence left unspoken, as at Jacob’s vow at Bethel, then shall the LORD be my God (Gen. 28:20-22). The name is played on twice: in v.9 it means he paineth, and here Jabez prays to be spared pain — the same delight in a name’s meaning that runs through the story of Isaac in Genesis, and a mark, Ellicott argues, of the section’s antiquity. And God granted, the text says — literally, God brought it to pass. The reformed John Gill adds the alternative the versions preserve: the Syriac and Arabic read the prayer in the third person, as the blessing his parents and friends wished upon him, though the Hebrew keeps it his own cry to the God of Israel.

The wesleyan-arminian witnesses press the devotion home. John Wesley thinks Jabez prayed thus when undertaking some great and dangerous service — enlarge my coast meaning, drive out these Canaanites whom God had bidden Israel root out — and that God prospered him remarkably in his studies, his business, and his conflicts. Adam Clarke turns the whole into a meditation: here is a man deeply conscious of his wants and therefore earnest, praying for the body’s needs and the soul’s together, dreading both sin and suffering and so praying against both; keep me from evil, that it may not grieve me, for we can never offend God without injuring ourselves. His prayer had a right aim, and so it had a right answer — and it was for this, Clarke concludes with the rest, and not for birth or title, that Jabez was counted more honourable than his brethren.
11:
A new branch opens with Chelub the brother of Shuah, father of Mehir and grandfather of Eshton, and the two witnesses divide gently over a single identification. The reformed John Gill reasons from a supposed equivalence: if Shuah is the same as the Hushah named a few verses back (v.4), then Chelub was the son of Ezer, and the new line hooks neatly onto the sons of Hur. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch decline that link. Chelub, they observe, is only another form of the name Caleb or Chelubai (1 Chron. 2:9), and is here marked off from the better-known Caleb the son of Hezron, and from the Caleb the son of Jephunneh who comes later (v.15), by the added clause the brother of Shuah. But Shuah is met with nowhere else, and the older commentators, they say, have without reason identified him with Hushah — so the neat connection Gill proposes is, on their reading, unwarranted.

The disagreement is small but real: is Shuah the Hushah of verse four, so that Chelub descends from Ezer and Hur, or is Shuah an otherwise unknown man whose brother Chelub belongs to the Calebite stock? Gill leans to the identification and the tidy descent; Keil and Delitzsch, distinguishing Chelub from the two famous Calebs and refusing to equate Shuah with Hushah, leave the branch’s exact attachment open, calling it simply the pedigree of the men of Rechah, whose connection with the larger families of Judah has not been handed down.

What both witnesses agree upon is the caution the genealogy demands. Names shift their forms — Chelub for Caleb, Chelubai for both — and a reader eager to tie every clan to a known ancestor may knit connections the text does not warrant. Keil and Delitzsch’s restraint and Gill’s tentative if are two ways of honouring the same limit: the line of Chelub and Mehir and Eshton is preserved as Judah’s, but just where it joins the trunk the record does not certainly say, and the witnesses will not pretend to a certainty the Chronicler withheld.
12:
Eshton’s sons are named — Bethrapha, Paseah, and Tehinnah the father of Irnahash — and the verse closes, these are the men of Rechah. The reformed John Gill offers a small gloss on the first name, taking Bethrapha to mean the family or house of Rapha. The wesleyan-arminian voices fasten instead on the closing phrase and its town. Adam Clarke reports the Targum’s bold paraphrase, which renders the men of Rechah as the men of the great Sanhedrin — turning a place-name into the supreme council of Israel. John Wesley reads the word as Rechab, and takes it soberly as the town from which the present inhabitants of Rechah are sprung, a place not elsewhere mentioned in Scripture.

The witnesses thus meet a familiar pattern: an obscure clan sealed to an obscure town, and the old interpreters reaching to give the name weight. The Targum’s identification with the Sanhedrin belongs to its habit, which the commentators note elsewhere in the chapter, of dignifying the men of these Judahite towns as doctors and councillors of the law; Clarke passes it on without endorsing it. Wesley’s quieter reading keeps to what can be known — that Rechah, or Rechab, was a settlement whose people descended from Eshton, and that nothing more of it survives.

So the verse adds another household to the branch of Chelub, and another town to the map of Judah’s clans. The men of Rechah rise here for a single naming and are gone; whether they were, as the Targum dreamed, a company of sages, or simply the folk of a forgotten village, the register does not say. The witnesses are content to record the names, mark the traditions that gathered round them, and let the small notice stand as one more thread in the fabric of the tribe — Eshton’s sons, the men of Rechah, numbered among the descendants of Judah and kept from vanishing altogether by a single line in the sacred roll. That so slight a notice should draw so grand a gloss from the Targum, and so plain a correction from Wesley, shows the two tempers the chapter meets everywhere — the one heaping honour on the obscure, the other keeping soberly to the little that is truly known.
13:
The register names the sons of Kenaz — Othniel and Seraiah — and here a figure of some fame steps briefly into the list. The reformed John Gill sets Kenaz in place as either the son of Chelub or of Eshton, tying him to the branch just traced. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch work out his deeper pedigree. Kenaz, they argue, was a descendant of Hezron the son of Pharez, as may be gathered from the fact that Caleb the son of Jephunneh, himself of Hezron’s line, is called a Kenizzite in Num. 32:12 and Josh. 14:6, and so was reckoned a descendant of Kenaz. Othniel and Seraiah, introduced as sons of Kenaz, are therefore not sons in the narrow sense but more distant descendants; for Othniel and Caleb the son of Jephunneh were brothers, as Josh. 15:17 and Judg. 1:13 attest.

The name of Othniel carries a memory the witnesses do not spell out here but have touched already. When the honour of Jabez was weighed a few verses back, Gill had rejected the rabbinic guess that Jabez was Othniel, noting that Othniel is spoken of in this very place as a distinct man; and so he is — set down plainly as a son of Kenaz, with his own son Hathath after him. The register keeps him in his proper rank in the Calebite line, neither merged with Jabez nor magnified beyond his notice.

The witnesses read the verse, then, as a small knot in which several threads meet: Kenaz reaching back to Hezron and Pharez, Othniel standing brother to the great Caleb, and the whole Kenizzite stock woven into the tribe of Judah though its origin lay, by the name Kenaz, close to the borders of Edom. Keil and Delitzsch’s careful reasoning — that sons of Kenaz must mean descendants, since Othniel was Caleb’s brother — is a specimen of how the genealogy must be read: not woodenly, generation by generation, but with an eye to the wider record, which alone shows how these names hang together in the house of Judah, and only so does the Kenizzite stock of Othniel find its true place within the tribe.
14:
The verse carries the line one step further and pauses on a striking name: Seraiah begat Joab, the father of the valley of Charashim, for they were craftsmen. The reformed John Gill notes Meonothai as another son of Othniel, and the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch add that Meonothai begat Ophrah, while Seraiah begat Joab, the father of the valley of craftsmen. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley reads the title in the same way: Joab was father, that is, chief or founder, of the inhabitants of the valley.

It is the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry who draws the lesson out. Here, he observes, is a whole family of craftsmen — handicraft tradesmen who applied themselves to all sorts of manufactures, and were so ingenious and industrious above their neighbours that the valley where they lived took its name from them, the valley of craftsmen. Henry will not have such men despised: those that are craftsmen, he insists, are not therefore to be looked upon as mean men. And he notes their good sense, that though two of a trade often disagree, these chose to live together, the better to improve their arts by comparing notes, and to support one another’s reputation.

The witnesses thus turn a bare pedigree into a small honour paid to skilled and honest labour. Where the register might have passed over these artisans as beneath notice, it fixes their valley in the record and names the founder from whom the guild sprang; and Henry, reading with the grain of the text, makes their industry a thing to be praised rather than scorned. Keil and Delitzsch keep the philology sober — Ophrah and Joab and the valley of Charashim are simply set in their places — but even their plain account preserves the same point: that the Chronicler counted the craftsmen of Judah worth remembering. The valley of craftsmen stands in the genealogy of the covenant tribe as a quiet witness that skill of hand, no less than rank or arms, has its place among the families of God’s people. Joab the founder is named, and his valley with him, that a whole guild of workmen might not be lost from the memory of Judah.
15:
Now comes the most celebrated name in the branch — Caleb the son of Jephunneh — and the witnesses greet the faithful spy with warmth while noting the tangles at the verse’s close. The reformed John Gill is careful about his ancestry: Jephunneh is not, as the Targum would have it, the same as Hezron, but another man, so that this Caleb is called the Kenizzite and stands as brother to Othniel; and he is the very Caleb who was sent as one of the spies of the land under Moses. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke recalls him from the wilderness narratives (Num. 14:24), where his courageous piety and inflexible integrity shone, and passes on the Targum’s play upon his name — that he was called Caleb because he had purged his soul from the counsel of the spies.

The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott sets the pedigree in its larger frame. Caleb the son of Jephunneh is called the Kenizzite in Josh. 14:6, and obtained his portion among the children of Judah (Josh. 15:13) because he wholly followed the LORD God of Israel (Josh. 14:14). If this Caleb and his clan were received among the Hezronite houses, Ellicott reasons, then this new division would thenceforth be known as the house of Caleb, or simply Caleb — the Chelubai of the earlier chapter. Elah, one of his sons here, had appeared as an Edomite princedom in 1 Chron. 1:52, as Kenaz had in the next verse there, a foreign clan taking the name of the people that adopted it.

The verse ends on a knot the witnesses cannot fully untie: the sons of Elah, even Kenaz. Ellicott lays out the possibilities — that Elah and Kenaz are two Calebite clans; that, with different vowel-points, Elah might be read these, so that the clause is a subscription, these are the sons of Kenaz, closing the list begun at verse thirteen; or that a name has simply dropped out before Kenaz. He leaves the question open. So the witnesses honour Caleb the Kenizzite as the great example of one who wholly followed the LORD and was grafted for it into the tribe of Judah, while owning honestly that the last words of his notice are broken, and that the exact roll of his sons cannot now be read with certainty.
16:
The sons of Jehaleleel — Ziph, Ziphah, Tiria, and Asareel — open a run of names whose place in the tribe the record no longer makes plain. The reformed John Gill supposes Jehaleleel to have been a son of the Uknaz just mentioned, hooking the new list to the last. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch are more guarded: these are descendants of various men whose genealogical connection with the sons and grandsons of Judah, named at the head of the chapter, is not given in the text as it has come down. Jehaleleel is a man not mentioned elsewhere; Ziph and Ziphah and the rest are met with only here; and there is, they judge, no strong reason to connect the name Ziph with the towns of that name in Josh. 15:24.

The other grammatical-historical witness, Charles Ellicott, is readier to see places behind the names. Jehaleleel means he praiseth God; Ziph he knows as one of the cities of Judah toward the border of Edom southward (Josh. 15:24), so that these clans too may have been Edomite Kenizzites; another Ziph, perhaps the Ziphah here, lay in the hill-country (Josh. 15:55). Asareel he takes as a dialectic form of Israel — a foreign clan, that is, adopting the name of the people among whom it had come to dwell.

So the two schools lean opposite ways over the same names: Keil and Delitzsch resist tying the clans to the towns, content to mark their connection as lost, while Ellicott reads town and clan together and even hears in Ziph and Asareel the traces of an Edomite-Kenizzite stock absorbed into Judah. Neither forces the evidence beyond what he thinks it bears. The witnesses leave the verse as a patch of the register where the threads run into obscurity — names once full of meaning to those who kept the rolls, now barely to be placed, yet preserved because they belonged, however dimly, to the families of Judah. Whether Keil and Delitzsch are right to leave the link unproven, or Ellicott to trace it toward Edom, the verse keeps its clans in the roll on the sole ground that the Chronicler received them there, and that ground the witnesses will not overturn.
17:
The sons of Ezra bring the register to one of its most disordered passages, and the witnesses face the corruption without flinching. The sons of Ezra were Jether, and Mered, and Epher, and Jalon — and then, abruptly, and she bare Miriam, and Shammai, and Ishbah, with no woman named as the subject of that she. The reformed John Gill guesses Ezra to have been a son of the Asareel just named. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch declare the verse, with the one that follows, quite unintelligible as it stands, showing clear traces of a corruption in the text: the name of the mother is wanting, and the sons of two wives of Mered are so tangled together that the account cannot be read straight. Of the sons themselves, Jether and Epher recur elsewhere (1 Chron. 5:24) but in other families, while Jalon is found only here.

Charles Ellicott, the other grammatical-historical voice, confirms the diagnosis and gathers the evidence for it. Some manuscripts read sons for the singular son; the name Ezra means help, the same as Ezer above; Jether had appeared as a Jerahmeelite (1 Chron. 2:32), Epher as a Manassite name. But the plain trouble is the dangling and she bare: who bore the three children is not clear from anything preceding, since the names before are all men’s. The Septuagint tries to mend it by reading and Jether bare Miriam, and the Syriac and Arabic simply omit both this verse and the next — which, Ellicott says, only confirms the suspicion that the text is faulty.

The witnesses thus give a clear-eyed lesson in textual honesty. They do not smooth the passage over or pretend to a sense it will not yield; they mark the missing subject, weigh the versions’ attempts to supply it, and name the corruption for what it is. That the register should reach the reader damaged here, its women lost and its clauses displaced, is owned frankly — and yet the commentators labour to recover what they can, because even a broken list of Judah’s households is worth the mending. The reconstruction the next verse will require is here begun, in the sober admission that the text, as it stands, cannot be right.
18:
The knot of the previous verse is untied here, and at its centre stands a marriage that startles the reader: an Israelite of Judah wedded to Bithiah the daughter of Pharaoh. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott shows how the tangle resolves. The sentence these are the sons of Bithiah the daughter of Pharaoh which Mered took, meaningless where it now stands, must be restored to its place after the opening of verse seventeen; then the house of Mered the son of Ezra divides cleanly into two, a purely Judahite group by his Jewish wife and a mixed Egyptian group by Bithiah. The Jewish wife — the margin’s the Jewess, set in deliberate contrast to the Egyptian — bore Jered the father of Gedor, Heber the father of Socho (Josh. 15:35), and Jekuthiel the father of Zanoah. Bithiah’s own name, daughter of Iah, Ellicott takes as the mark of a convert to the God of Israel; and daughter of Pharaoh, if the naming be tribal, may mean only an Egyptian clan that had joined itself to Mered’s.

The witnesses divide over just how to read that royal bride. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry takes her plainly as a princess of Egypt, Pharaoh being the common name of its kings, and supposes the marriage to have fallen in the days before the bondage began, while Joseph’s merits were still fresh — so that it is no marvel an Israelite should then be preferred to be the king’s son-in-law. The reformed John Gill reads Jehudijah simply as another wife, a Jewess named so to distinguish her from the Egyptian in the latter part of the verse. And the wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke reports the Targum’s wholly allegorical flight, which turns every name into a title of Moses and makes Bithiah the daughter of Pharaoh his foster-mother, a proselyte whom Mered — that is, Caleb — took to wife; a paraphrase Clarke relays as a curiosity rather than as history.

So the debate is real, though gently held: was Bithiah a literal Egyptian princess married into Judah before the oppression, as Henry thinks; a converted daughter of Iah or an Egyptian clan absorbed into Mered’s house, as Ellicott reasons; or the veiled foster-mother of Moses, as the Targum dreamed? The witnesses do not settle it, but they agree on what the verse quietly declares — that the line of Judah could take in even a daughter of Pharaoh, whether by a true marriage or by the grafting-in of a foreign clan, and count her sons among its own. The register that guards the covenant blood is not, after all, closed against the stranger who is joined to Israel.
19:
Another wife’s sons are recorded, and once more a small ambiguity of the Hebrew draws the witnesses’ notice. The reformed John Gill reads the verse of Hodiah, another wife of Mered — though he reports that Hillerus took her to be the same as the Jehudijah of the verse before, while others read the words differently, taking Hodiah as the name of a man, so that the sense would be the sons of the wife of Hodiah. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott notes that the existing Hebrew does indeed say the sons of Hodiah’s wife, and that Hodiah recurs elsewhere as a man’s name (Neh. 8:7); but a very slight change, the adding of three letters, would give instead the sons of his Jewish wife, the sister of Naham, were the father of Keilah the Garmite, and Eshtemoa.

Ellicott sets the places in view. Naham is unknown; Keilah is the town in the lowland (Josh. 15:44) famous as the scene of David’s rescue and peril (1 Sam. 23:1); Eshtemoa had been named already in the list. The Garmites and Maachathites are clans otherwise unknown, the former settled at Keilah; and the word father of seems to have dropped out before Eshtemoa, the sense being that the Maachathites were seated there. The whole notice, he adds, is still dealing with the Calebite division of Hezron, for Maachah was named earlier as a concubine of Caleb.

The witnesses thus keep the reader’s footing on uncertain ground. Was Hodiah a woman, the wife of Mered, or a man whose wife bore these sons? Gill lays both readings side by side; Ellicott, preferring the emended text, makes her the Jewish wife and sister of Naham. Neither pretends the Hebrew is unambiguous. But under the small textual question the same larger fact holds steady: these clans — of Keilah and Eshtemoa, Garmite and Maachathite — belong to the house of Caleb within Judah, and the register, though its wording here is worn, keeps them fastened in their place among the tribe, so that Keilah of David’s wars and Eshtemoa of the hills stand recorded as households of the covenant people.
20:
The sons of Shimon are named — Amnon, Rinnah, Benhanan, and Tilon — with the sons of Ishi, Zoheth and Benzoheth, appended after them. The witnesses find little to fasten on, and confess it. The reformed John Gill supposes Shimon to have been yet another son of Mered by his last wife, or perhaps the same as the Shammai named above. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read Benhanan as a single name, note that Ishi occurs elsewhere in the chapter and in the earlier register though never joined to a Zoheth, and observe that after Benzoheth the names of the sons are simply wanting, the list breaking off.

Charles Ellicott, the other grammatical-historical voice, admits that nothing is said elsewhere of these men or of the sons of Ishi. Ishi had appeared as a Jerahmeelite name (1 Chron. 2:31); but since throughout this whole section the ramifications of the house of Caleb have plainly been the subject, and since this verse is appended to the rest without any note to the contrary, it is highly probable, he reasons, that it too refers to some Calebite clans and towns. The connection is inferred rather than stated, drawn from the drift of the passage rather than from any word of the text.

The witnesses thus read the verse as one more stretch of names carried on the momentum of the list. They will not invent a lineage for Shimon or Ishi where none is given, but neither do they cut these households loose; the reasonable presumption, Ellicott argues and the others do not gainsay, is that they belong with the Calebite families among whom they stand. So the sons of Shimon and of Ishi are kept in the register on the strength of their company — numbered with the clans of Caleb in Judah because that is the stock the whole passage has been tracing, even where, as here, the record grows too thin to say so outright. It is a fitting close to the long Judahite section: a last handful of names, their fathers uncertain and their towns unnamed, held in the register by nothing but their place beside the house of Caleb — and that, the witnesses judge, is warrant enough to keep them among the sons of Judah.
21:
The register now leaves the lines of Pharez and Zerah and turns to Judah’s third son: the sons of Shelah the son of Judah. The reformed John Gill explains the order — the posterity of Judah by Pharez has been given at length, and rightly so, for the honour of David and of his kingdom, and of the King Messiah, sprang from that line; now the writer returns to give account of Shelah, Judah’s son by the daughter of Shuah. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch mark a nicety of the Hebrew: the little conjunction that had linked family to family through the whole preceding list is dropped before the sons of Shelah, to show that these form a second line of descent, co-ordinate with the sons of Judah already named. Er is here father of Lecah, and Laadah father of Mareshah — a town, they note, still traceable as the ruin Marash in the lowland (Josh. 15:44).

The verse closes on a household honoured for its craft: the families of the house of them that wrought fine linen, of the house of Ashbea. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke passes on the Targum’s gloss, that these were the family who worked in fine flax to make garments for kings and priests, while John Wesley notes only that the writer, having treated Judah’s progeny by Pharez and Zerah, now comes to his line by Shelah.

It is the puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry who makes the linen-weavers a matter of honour. It is inserted in their genealogy as their credit, he says, that they were the best weavers in the kingdom, and that they brought up their children from one generation to the next to the same business, not aiming to make them gentlemen. As before the flood one man was called the father of musicians and another the father of shepherds, so Laadah is named the father of those that wrought fine linen; and their posterity kept to the city of Mareshah, whose staple was the linen-cloth with which kings and priests were clothed. The witnesses thus close Shelah’s notice as they had closed the craftsmen’s valley — with a quiet dignity laid upon honest and inherited labour, the weavers of Judah written into the sacred register beside its princes and its spies.
22:
The line of Shelah runs on into names half-lost and a memory of former greatness: Jokim, the men of Chozeba, Joash and Saraph who had the dominion in Moab, and Jashubilehem — and these are ancient things. The reformed John Gill can do little more than mark where the Vulgate parts from the Hebrew, the verse resisting a clean rendering. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch read it with the next, and puzzle over how far the description reaches — whether the potters and gardeners of the following verse are all the sons of Shelah or only those just named — inclining to take it of all the households here except the linen-weavers already singled out.

The wesleyan-arminian voices dwell on the dominion in Moab and its bitter sequel. John Wesley takes it that these men ruled in Moab in the name and for the use of the kings of Judah, to whom Moab was subject from David’s time; and he hears in these are ancient things a sigh — those blessed days are long since past, for the ancestors who once held dominion over the heathen have degenerate posterity now enslaved in Chaldea, set to servile work as potters and gardeners. Adam Clarke relays the Targum’s fancy, which weaves Joash and Saraph into the story of Ruth, identifying them with Mahlon and Chilion who took wives of the daughters of Moab.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers the whole into a meditation on the turns of providence. It was found among the ancient things that this family once had dominion in Moab — probably, he thinks, transplanting themselves thither when David conquered that country, and holding places of power there for several generations, though it was all a great while ago, time out of mind. Their posterity, now, are brought low. Henry’s warning is plain: those who at present have dominion know not to what their children may be reduced, nor what mean employments they may one day be glad to take up. The witnesses leave the verse poised between a proud past and a servile present — a household of Judah that had ruled in Moab, remembered now only as ancient things, its heirs scattered and humbled in a foreign land.
23:
The households of Shelah end among the workshops: these were the potters, and those that dwelt among plants and hedges; there they dwelt with the king for his work. The reformed John Gill reads it as a come-down — the posterity of men once famous are now of mean employments, some of them making earthen pots. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke, after relaying the Targum’s grand paraphrase that would make these potters the disciples of the law for whose sake the world was created, confesses the gloss gives as little light as the text itself, and settles for the plain sense: they were probably brickmakers, perhaps potters too, who dwelt in low grounds and worked the clay dug up in forming the fences of the king’s domains.

Here the witnesses genuinely part over two phrases. On those that dwelt among plants and hedges, the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott would read proper names: not hedges and plants in general, but the inhabitants of Netaim and Gederah — Netaim meaning plantations, and Gederah a town in the lowland (Josh. 15:36) — recalling that Solomon had pleasure-gardens near Bethlehem and Uzziah his farms and vineyards (2 Chron. 26:10). And on the king, the schools divide outright. Ellicott takes it of a king of Judah, the potteries of Netaim and Gederah being a royal establishment, as those of Sèvres once were. But John Wesley, reading the verse through the eyes of the exile, takes the king to be the king of Babylon, and the whole notice as a rebuke: these were mean spirits who would rather tarry among the heathen in the meanest employments than return to Jerusalem to serve God and enjoy their freedom, esteeming it a greater honour to serve an earthly monarch than the King of kings in his temple.

So the debate is drawn between two readings of the same clause. Was the household’s dwelling with the king an honour — a royal pottery in the land of Judah, working for a king of David’s line — or a shame, the drudgery of captives in Babylon who had forgotten the way home? Ellicott’s philology points to the former, Wesley’s and Gill’s moralizing to the latter, with Clarke hovering between, seeing only humble brickmakers about the king’s fences. The witnesses do not resolve it; they leave the potters of Shelah’s line at their wheels, and the reader to weigh whether their place beside the king was a station of trust or a badge of exile.
24:
The register crosses now from Judah to Simeon, and the witnesses first explain the neighbourly order. The reformed John Gill notes that Simeon is set next to Judah, before Reuben the elder, because Simeon’s inheritance lay within the territory of Judah; his first son is Nemuel, the same as the Jemuel of the older lists. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch compare the parallels closely: Genesis and Exodus name six sons of Simeon, but here, as in Num. 26:12, only five are given, for Ohad’s posterity had either died out or dwindled past founding a family. Of the five, Jarib answers to the Jachin of Numbers — a copyist’s slip, they judge, of one letter — while Nemuel and Zerah are the Jemuel and Zohar of Genesis, the same men under variant names.

The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke throws up his hands at the variations: the genealogy is very different from those in Genesis and Numbers, occasioned probably by the same person bearing several names; to reconcile them is impossible, and to attempt it useless. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott, by contrast, reconciles them patiently — Genesis and Exodus give six, Numbers and Chronicles five, Ohad being dropped; Jarib stands for Jachin, Nemuel and Zerah for Jemuel and Zohar — and adds the telling detail that Shaul was the son of a Canaanitess (Gen. 46:10), whose mixed line, he observes, was the only Simeonite clan that grew populous.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry lifts his eyes from the names to the tribe’s whole story, which the chapter will unfold: Simeon was never a tribe of great note; it increased, but not like Judah, and those whom God increases ought to be thankful even when they see others more increased. So the witnesses set the sons of Simeon in place with two tempers side by side — Clarke despairing of the tangled lists and letting them lie, Keil and Ellicott untangling them thread by thread — and both, in their way, honest before the difficulty. What none disputes is the register’s purpose: to fasten Simeon, the lesser brother lodged inside Judah’s borders, into the great enrollment of the tribes, and to prepare, in that mixed and dwindling house, for the small remnant whose fortunes the rest of the chapter will trace.
25:
A single thread of Simeon’s house is drawn out through six generations, beginning here: Shallum his son, Mibsam his son, Mishma his son. The reformed John Gill sets the sequence plainly — Shallum the son of Shaul, Mibsam the son of Shallum, Mishma the son of Mibsam — a straight descent, name handing on to name. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch fix the head of the line: Shallum was without doubt the son of the last-named Shaul, the same who in Genesis and Exodus is marked out as the son of a Canaanitish woman, and so distinguished from Simeon’s other sons. It is this mixed line, they note, that the register now traces down through six generations to a certain Shimei.

Keil and Delitzsch observe, too, a small seam in the list. It is broken into two groups by the words and the sons of Mishma at the opening of the next verse, though the reason for the division is not known; perhaps Mishma and his descendants, Hammuel and Zacchur and Shimei, together with the fathers and sons already named, were reckoned as forming a single larger family. The plural, sons of Mishma, they take to gather up that whole further stretch of the pedigree.

The witnesses read the verse, then, as the quiet beginning of Simeon’s one flourishing branch. Out of a tribe that would never be great, and out of a son born to a Canaanite mother, the Chronicler follows a line that did multiply, tracing it link by link toward the Shimei whose many children the coming verses will count. There is no story here, only succession — but the succession is the point, for it is this mixed and unpromising stock, faithfully enrolled, that will supply the princes and the settlers whose deeds close the chapter. Gill and Keil and Delitzsch are content to lay the first three links in place and let the line run on. Neither reaches for a lesson the text will not carry; they simply establish that this branch, unlike its barren kinsmen, was chosen to be followed, because at its far end stood a household that grew, and out of that household the men whose enterprise would give the chapter its one stretch of story.
26:
The line runs on: the sons of Mishma; Hamuel his son, Zacchur his son, Shimei his son. The reformed John Gill names them simply — Hamuel, Zacchur, and Shimei — carrying the descent one stage further toward its flowering. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch, who had marked at the previous verse how the pedigree divides into two groups, gather the second group here: from Mishma came Hamuel, and from him Zacchur, and from him Shimei, in whom the branch reaches the man of many children. For Shimei, they note, had sixteen sons and six daughters, and by them became the father of a numerous race — while his brethren, the other Simeonites, had not many sons, so that the tribe as a whole never multiplied to the measure of Judah, a fact borne out by the numberings under Moses.

The witnesses thus use the verse to bring Simeon’s single fruitful line to its head. All the register’s patience through the barren-seeming names now pays off: the descent that began with Shaul the son of a Canaanite woman arrives at Shimei, whose crowded household will stand in sharp relief against the thinness of the rest of the tribe. Keil and Delitzsch draw the contrast that the next verse will state outright — one man richly increased, his kinsmen barely holding their own.

So the verse, bare as it looks, is the hinge of Simeon’s story. The Chronicler has followed the mixed line down six generations for the sake of what stands at its end, and the witnesses, tracing Hamuel and Zacchur and Shimei, keep the reader moving toward the point the genealogy has been quietly making — that even in a small and scattered tribe, God had raised up one house that multiplied, out of which the princes and pioneers of the chapter’s close would come. The line is thin, but it is not broken, and at Shimei it broadens at last. Gill contents himself with the three names; Keil and Delitzsch look past them to the crowded household ahead, and to the pointed contrast the register is building — the one house that flourished set against the many that failed, so that even Simeon’s smallness serves to magnify the mercy that raised up a fruitful branch within it.
27:
Now the register states outright what its patient tracing has implied: Shimei had sixteen sons and six daughters, but his brethren had not many children, neither did all their family multiply, like to the children of Judah. The reformed John Gill notes only that Shimei’s many children are nowhere named. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke sets the numbers side by side: at the first census the tribe of Simeon stood at fifty-nine thousand three hundred and Judah at seventy-four thousand six hundred; but by the second numbering Judah had risen to seventy-six thousand five hundred while Simeon had fallen to twenty-two thousand two hundred, a decrease of more than thirty-seven thousand — by then the smallest tribe in Israel.

The witnesses hear behind these figures an old word of doom. The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley ascribes the tribe’s failure to increase to the curse Jacob pronounced upon Simeon (Gen. 49:5-7), and to Moses’s passing him by when he blessed the other tribes. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the same history in the verse: his brethren are the fellow-tribesmen, the other Simeonite clans, and the sense is that they did not multiply their whole tribe; Simeon was never historically important and was in the end all but absorbed by Judah, its domain lying within Judah’s own — the very fulfilment of the patriarch’s sentence, I will divide them in Jacob, and scatter them in Israel (Gen. 49:7).

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry draws the gentler lesson. Simeon increased greatly in Shimei’s house, yet not like the children of Judah; and those whom God increases, he says, ought to be thankful, even when they see others more increased than themselves. So the witnesses read the verse as both judgment and instruction. The stunted growth of Simeon is traced by Wesley and Ellicott straight to Jacob’s curse of scattering, worked out over centuries in shrinking census-rolls and a lost inheritance; and yet Henry will not let the smaller tribe despair, hearing even here a call to gratitude. The word spoken over Simeon at the patriarch’s deathbed stands visibly accomplished in these numbers — a people divided and diminished — while the promise that ran to Judah swells beside it, the two destinies set in the same verse for the reader to weigh.
28:
The tribe’s dwelling-places are now enrolled: they dwelt at Beersheba, and Moladah, and Hazarshual, and the towns that follow. The reformed John Gill observes that these are set down in nearly the same order, and with little variation of names, as in the older allotment. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch identify them as the ancient dwelling-places the Simeonites received within the tribal domain of Judah when Joshua divided the land (Josh. 19:1) — eighteen cities in all, ranged in two groups of thirteen and five, exactly as in the book of Joshua. They note the small discrepancies between the lists: a name, Sheba, standing in Joshua between Beersheba and Moladah that is absent here, and various towns appearing under differing forms or under wholly different names.

Charles Ellicott, the other grammatical-historical voice, presses the same comparison. Joshua adds Sheba after Beersheba, which he takes to be a mistaken repetition, since the total is given as thirteen cities; Beersheba he fixes as the modern Bir-esseba, Moladah as a site south of Hebron, while Hazarshual, the fox-village, remains unknown. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry simply marks that the cities allotted to Simeon are here recorded, as they had been in Joshua’s day.

The witnesses thus read the verse as the register turning from bloodlines to boundaries, enrolling the towns where Simeon lived among the fields of Judah. The very fact that these places must be sought in Judah’s territory, and that their names have drifted and doubled between the two lists, tells its own story — the tribe was lodged inside its stronger brother from the first, its cities half-shared and its record kept only by careful comparison with the older allotment. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott do that work of comparison exactly, matching town to town and marking each difference, so that even Simeon’s scattered settlements are held fast in the sacred record, numbered as they had been when Joshua parcelled out the land. That the Chronicler should trouble to copy out these towns again, long after many had passed from the tribe’s hands, is itself a witness to his purpose — to keep Simeon a distinct and remembered people, with a map of its own, though it dwelt on borrowed ground.
32:
Set apart from the thirteen towns are five more — Etam, and Ain, Rimmon, and Tochen, and Ashan, five cities — and the witnesses ask why these five stand by themselves. The reformed John Gill notes that only four are named in the older list, one having perhaps been added since or newly built, namely Tochen; and he reports Kimchi’s view that these were all that remained to the Simeonites to dwell in by David’s day, so that they were driven to seek new settlements and pasture elsewhere. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott confirms both points. The old Jewish expositors Rashi and Kimchi held that whereas the thirteen cities were lost to Simeon from David’s time, these five remained in the tribe’s possession — which is why the register, like the book of Joshua before it, sets them apart.

Ellicott adds his customary care over the names and numbers. Joshua counts four here — Ain, Rimmon, Ether, and Ashan — where our text has five; Etam may be a slip for Ether, though there were in fact two towns called Etam, and Ain and Rimmon are elsewhere spoken of almost as one place, so close did they lie. Tochen, he observes, is found only here.

The witnesses thus read the little cluster of five as a remnant deliberately marked off. When the greater number of Simeon’s cities had passed away — lost to David, or forfeited, or overrun — these five were what the tribe still held, its shrunken portion within the land of Judah. Gill and Ellicott, leaning both on the medieval Jewish commentators, agree that the separation is not accidental but meaningful: the register distinguishes what was lost from what was kept. And it is precisely because the tribe’s holdings had so dwindled — five villages and their pastures — that the Simeonites would presently go seeking room beyond their borders, as the closing verses of the chapter will tell. So the five cities, small as the number is, are made to carry a whole history: they are what remained when the rest was gone, the last foothold of a shrinking tribe, and the very smallness of the holding is the pressure that will drive the princes of Simeon out to Gedor and to Seir in search of pasture.
33:
The account of Simeon’s dwellings is sealed: all their villages that were round about the same cities, unto Baal. These were their habitations, and their genealogy. The reformed John Gill identifies the boundary-point, taking Baal to be the same as Baalathbeer. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch compare the parallel (Josh. 19:8), where the place is more fully given as Baalathbeer, Ramah of the south, and grant that its exact position has not been certainly ascertained. But their interest falls on the closing words: these are their dwelling-places, and their family register was to them — meaning that, small tribe though they were and lodged in the very midst of Judah, the Simeonites yet kept a genealogical register of their own.

Charles Ellicott reads the ending the same way. Unto Baal he glosses as Baalathbeer, the lady of the well, adding that the parallel names this cluster of villages Ramath-negeb, the Ramah of the southland (1 Sam. 30:27). And the final clause he renders, they had their own registration — that is, though their settlements lay within the territory of Judah, their clans were enrolled as belonging to a distinct tribe.

The witnesses converge on this quiet but telling point. Simeon had no separate land of its own; its towns were Judah’s towns, its villages scattered through Judah’s south. Yet it kept its own register, its own reckoning of descent, and so remained a distinct people even while dwelling inside another. Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott alike hear in that closing phrase the very theme of the whole chapter — that identity is preserved by the keeping of the record. The tribe that Jacob had sentenced to be scattered held its place among the tribes not by territory but by genealogy, by the faithful enrollment of its households; and the Chronicler, setting down these were their habitations, and their genealogy, gives Simeon exactly that — a name and a register, when it no longer had a land to call its own. It is the fitting seal upon the whole account of the tribe: dwellings shared, borders blurred, cities lost, yet the genealogy kept intact, so that Simeon endures in the book of God precisely as a distinct people, remembered by its descent when its ground had all but disappeared beneath Judah’s.
34:
The chapter turns from lists of towns to a company of living men — Meshobab, and Jamlech, and Joshah the son of Amaziah — the first of thirteen princes whose exploits will close the chapter. The reformed John Gill introduces them as famous men in the tribe of Simeon, of rank and dignity, and eminent for courage and valour, as the latter part of the chapter will show, though they are nowhere else in Scripture taken notice of; he adds the small curiosity that the name Jamlech is not far from Jamblichus, the name of a famous Platonic philosopher. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch set the whole passage in view: these verses record the emigrations of Simeonite families and an expedition of conquest undertaken in the days of Hezekiah, and the thirteen princes here enumerated were its leaders. Some of their pedigrees are traced through several generations, they note, yet in no case far enough to show how they connect with the families named earlier in the tribe.

The witnesses thus read the verse as the muster-roll of a bold venture. After the long enrollment of dwindling clans and half-lost towns, the register suddenly names men of courage — princes who would lead their people out to seek room and pasture beyond the crowded borders of Judah. Keil and Delitzsch’s observation, that their genealogies cannot be linked to the earlier lists, is itself instructive: these are heads of households risen to prominence in their own generation, remembered for what they did rather than for whom they descended from.

So Meshobab and Jamlech and their fellows step onto the page as the actors of the chapter’s one stretch of narrative. Gill’s note that they are honoured here though unmentioned elsewhere catches the pathos and the dignity of it — men of real valour, known to God and to the Chronicler, whose single recorded deed is the enterprise now to be told. The verse opens the roll of those who, out of a small and scattered tribe, would carry Simeon’s name into new lands in the reign of Hezekiah, and gives the register, at its close, the actors it has long been waiting to name.
38:
The muster-roll is now sealed with its summary: these mentioned by their names were princes in their families, and the house of their fathers increased greatly. The reformed John Gill reads it plainly — these were the principal men among the Simeonites, the heads of their fathers’ houses. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch render the same words, these mentioned by their names were princes in their families, whose fathers’-houses had increased to a multitude, and take the notice as the ground of what follows: it was because these households had multiplied, and grown too large for their old holdings, that they went out — the verb of departure standing next in the text — to seek new room and pasture.

The witnesses thus find in the summary the hinge between genealogy and history. The whole long list of names, from Meshobab onward, is here certified: these were no obscure stragglers but the recognized chiefs of Simeon, and their fathers’ houses had swelled greatly. That very increase, Keil and Delitzsch observe, is what drives the narrative forward — a growing people in a shrunken land must look beyond its borders, and the princes just named will lead them.

So the verse gathers up the thirteen and points them outward. Where the earlier notice had lamented that Simeon as a whole did not multiply like Judah, here at least some houses of the tribe are said to have increased greatly — enough to press against the narrow bounds of their inheritance and to send out companies of conquest. Gill’s plain gloss and Keil and Delitzsch’s fuller rendering meet in the same sense: these were the princes, their families were many, and their very fruitfulness is the reason the chapter now turns from lists to the story of an expedition. The register has named its actors and stated their motive; the deeds themselves come next. There is a quiet irony the witnesses do not miss: the tribe that Jacob doomed to be scattered is here saved by a scattering of its own, its increase forcing it outward into new lands. What in the patriarch’s mouth had been a curse becomes, in the pressure of a growing house upon a narrow inheritance, the very spur to the enterprise that will carry Simeon’s name abroad and preserve a remnant of it in days to come.
39:
The Simeonite princes set out to find grazing for their flocks: they went to the entrance of Gedor, even unto the east side of the valley, to seek pasture. Here the witnesses divide over a point of Hebrew. The reformed John Gill takes the entrance of Gedor as a place, distinct from the Gedor in Judah and lying further off — perhaps the Gedaris that ancient geographers set among the Philistine cities — inhabited, as the next verse will say, by the posterity of Ham. The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry reads it likewise as a locality, a place seemingly in Arabia called the entrance of Gedor, which the Simeonites seized and settled.

The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch dissent. The phrase, they argue, does not mean the entrance of Gedor at all; since the answering word in the verse means the rising of the sun, that is, the east, the term it balances must denote the west, being shortened from a fuller expression for sunset. So they render, they went westward from Gedor to the east side of the valley. And they rule out the Gedor of the highlands of Judah, north of Hebron (Josh. 15:58), on two grounds: there is no open valley there such as the passage requires, and the Simeonites could not have waged a war of conquest within the territory of Judah in the reign of Hezekiah. Just where this Gedor lay, they confess, cannot now be determined.

The disagreement is genuine, turning on a single ambiguous word: is the phrase a proper name, the entrance of Gedor, marking the town the raiders reached, as Gill and Henry hold; or a compass-point, the west of Gedor, describing the direction they traveled, as Keil and Delitzsch insist? The witnesses do not resolve it. But they agree on the substance the verse reports — that in Hezekiah’s day a people cramped for room went out beyond their borders in search of pasture, and found it in a valley held by others. Whether they marched to Gedor’s gate or to the country west of it, the Simeonites were seeking, and about to seize, the grazing-land that the following verses describe.
40:
The seekers find what they sought: fat pasture and good, and the land was wide, and quiet, and peaceable; for they of Ham had dwelt there of old. The reformed John Gill sets the discovery in or near the valley of Gedor. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott savours the words: the land was wide — literally broad on both hands, an open plain; and quiet and peaceable, like Laish which the Danites once took by surprise (Judg. 18:7); while they of Ham means that those dwelling there were Hamites, or Canaanites (1 Chron. 1:8), settled in the valley from of old, before ever the Simeonite invasion came.

The witnesses differ mildly over who these peaceable folk were. Adam Clarke, the wesleyan-arminian, guesses them Philistines or Egyptians dwelling at Gedor, distinguishing them from the Amalekites whom another band of Simeonites would smite in Seir. Keil and Delitzsch take them for Hamites, and note that with them dwelt also the Meunites, nomad strangers connected with a city near Petra, whom the invaders utterly destroyed — the old word of the ban, they observe, here used of total extermination.

The wesleyan-arminian John Wesley draws the devotion out. Those who dwell, as these did, in a fruitful country, whose land is wide and quiet and peaceable, have reason to own themselves indebted to the God who appoints the bounds of men’s habitation; and he adds the ground of the conquest — that the valley lay not in the hands of Simeon’s brethren of Judah, but of a people whom Israel had authority to expel. So the witnesses read the verse both as history and as providence. A cramped tribe finds a rich and undefended land, and takes it; and behind the taking stands the God who allots to every people its dwelling, giving to Simeon the pastures of Ham. The peace of the valley proved its undoing, as the peace of Laish had; and the fat and quiet land, held by the sons of Ham from of old, passed by the ban into the hands of the sons of Simeon.
41:
The blow falls in the reign of the good king: these written by name came in the days of Hezekiah king of Judah, and smote their tents, and the habitations that were found there, and destroyed them utterly unto this day, and dwelt in their rooms. The reformed John Gill identifies the actors simply as the princes written by name a few verses before. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott fills in the detail. The victims lived in tents, for they were nomads; and the word rendered habitations stands in the margin as the Meunites, a very similar word in Hebrew, which the Greek confirms — sojourners, that is, from Maon south of the Dead Sea, near Petra. To destroy utterly is to devote to God for destruction, as Jericho was devoted (Josh. 6:17); and this practice, Ellicott notes, was not peculiar to Israel but common among the Semitic peoples — Mesha king of Moab, on his own inscribed stone, boasts of having devoted seven thousand of the men of Nebo to his god Chemosh.

Keil and Delitzsch read the conquest the same way, dwelling on that word of the ban — the term for smiting with the curse, they observe, which had gradually lost its original religious force and come to mean simply utter destruction. The Simeonite princes fell upon the peaceable Hamites and the Meunites among them, wiped them out, and took their place.

The witnesses thus set the episode in a sober and terrible light. The phrase unto this day, Ellicott adds, marks the time when the record was first written, long before the Chronicler borrowed it from his sources — so that the notice comes from close to the events themselves. What it records is a holy war in miniature: a people devoted to destruction, their pastures seized, their tents burned, in the name of the God to whom the spoil was given up. The witnesses neither soften the ban nor make Israel unique in it; they set the Simeonite conquest, with its cross-reference to Jericho and its parallel on the Moabite Stone, within the hard world of the ancient Near East, where a land was taken by devoting its people to a god — here, to the LORD, in the days of Hezekiah.
42:
A second venture is told: some of them, even of the sons of Simeon, five hundred men, went to mount Seir, under four captains — Pelatiah, Neariah, Rephaiah, and Uzziel, the sons of Ishi. The reformed John Gill sets the scene in the land of Edom. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch describe a distinct war of conquest: led by four chiefs of the sons of Shimei, five hundred men marched to Mount Seir, smote the remnant of the Amalekites who had escaped earlier destruction, and dwelt there to this day. From the leaders being sons of Shimei, they infer that the whole band belonged to that flourishing family; and the escaped of Amalek they identify as those who had survived the victories of Saul and David over that hereditary enemy (1 Sam. 15:7).

Charles Ellicott weighs the timing and the connection. The verb, he notes, may be read had gone; the expedition’s date is not stated, but for that very reason it is likely to have been nearly contemporaneous with the conquest of Gedor just described, and the band of five hundred probably belonged to the same clans that had smitten the Hamites. Neither Ishi nor his sons are otherwise known. Were a wholly separate enterprise intended, Ellicott argues, the phrase of them, of the sons of Simeon would be a needlessly misleading way of saying simply some of the sons of Simeon; the of them can only point back to the clans whose emigration in Hezekiah’s days has been the subject all along.

The witnesses thus read the verse as the second prong of a single movement. The same restless, multiplying households that seized the valley of Ham sent out another company, smaller and southward, against the mountain fastness of Edom, to fall upon the last of Amalek. Keil and Delitzsch fix the band’s descent from Shimei, Ellicott its likely nearness in time to the first raid, and both keep it firmly within the Hezekian expansion the chapter has been recounting. Five hundred men of a small tribe, led by four brothers, march into Seir — and the ancient feud with Amalek, begun in the wilderness, moves toward its close.
43:
The chapter ends with a long feud finished: they smote the rest of the Amalekites that were escaped, and dwelt there unto this day. The reformed John Gill recalls the earlier judgments the survivors had slipped through — the sword of Saul, who by Jewish reckoning slew great numbers of them in a day, and the sword of David after him.
