The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the phrase precisely: it is the remnant of the survivors of Amalek, those who had taken refuge in Seir from the exterminating wars of Saul and David (1 Sam. 15:7), and who appear, by the older genealogy, as a partly Edomite stock (1 Chron. 1:36). The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke agrees, and adds from Calmet that this expedition fell near the time of the captivity of the ten tribes, when the remnant of Simeon, retiring southward into Arabia for fear of the Jews, would have pressed on into Edom.

The puritan-evangelical Matthew Henry gathers the chapter’s threads into its closing lesson. In this last stroke, he says, the old curses upon Ham and upon Amalek found a further accomplishment, when they seemed long dormant; and the curse upon Simeon himself — that he should be divided and scattered in Israel (Gen. 49:7) — was here turned into a blessing. For these families of Simeon, transplanting themselves into distant countries, are said to dwell there unto this day, and so, Henry thinks, escaped the calamities of the captivity that swept away their brethren; Providence sometimes sends those out of trouble whom it designs for preservation.

So the register closes where a book of judgment might, on a note of grave mercy. The hereditary enemy of God’s people, Amalek, whom Saul had spared and David had struck, is at last extirpated by five hundred men of the least of the tribes; and the very scattering that Jacob had pronounced over Simeon becomes, in God’s hand, the means of that tribe’s rescue. The witnesses leave the chapter as they found it — a dry genealogy — but one through which, at the end, the old promises and the old curses are seen working themselves out, so that a list of forgotten names becomes a testimony that God keeps his word, in judgment upon Amalek and in the preservation of his own.
29:
The roll of Simeon’s towns continues — at Bilhah, and at Ezem, and at Tolad — and here only the grammatical-historical witnesses are at hand, matching each name against the older records. Keil and Delitzsch carry the list forward toward its close, where the five villages of Etam, Ain, Rimmon, Tochen, and Ashan will be reckoned, against the four of the parallel in Joshua; they weigh the conjectures of earlier scholars about how the counts came to differ, whether by a name dropped in one list or doubled in another, and find the proposed emendations unsupported by the actual order of the names.

Charles Ellicott sets these towns on the map of the far south. Many of the places assigned to Simeon here, he observes, are reckoned among the cities of the extreme south of Judah in the book of Joshua (Josh. 15:26); Bilhah, or Balah, is perhaps the Baalah named there, and Ezem — the Azem of the older text — and Eltolad are mentioned in the same company, though their exact sites are now unknown.

The witnesses read the verse, as they must, through the lens of comparison, for the names themselves yield no story. What the patient matching shows is the deep truth of Simeon’s settlement: town after town lies in the southern reaches of Judah’s own inheritance, so that the tribe’s home is written into its stronger brother’s map. The differences between this list and Joshua’s — a Balah for a Bilhah, an Azem for an Ezem — are the ordinary drift of names across parallel registers, and Keil and Delitzsch and Ellicott treat them so, neither hiding the variations nor making more of them than they bear. The obscure villages of Simeon are kept in the record by just this care, each fixed as nearly as may be in the arid south where the tribe had its portion, that no dwelling-place of the covenant people might fall out of the reckoning. Bilhah and Ezem and Tolad are names and little more, their sites now lost; but the two witnesses, refusing either to guess beyond the evidence or to let the towns go unremarked, do for them what a genealogist can — set them beside the older list, note where the forms have shifted, and keep them lodged in the record of Simeon’s inheritance within Judah.
35:
The muster continues — Joel, and Jehu the son of Josibiah, the son of Seraiah, the son of Asiel — and here the grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch carry the reading almost alone, attending to the pedigrees and to the formula that will gather these men up. Jehu’s descent is traced back three generations, through Josibiah and Seraiah to Asiel, a fuller lineage than most of the princes receive; and Keil and Delitzsch mark how the whole company will presently be summed up in the phrase these mentioned by their names were princes in their families, whose fathers’-houses had increased to a multitude.

They dwell, with their usual precision, on the very words of that summary. The Hebrew expression, they explain, means literally those who have come with their names — that is, those who have been expressly mentioned by name — and they distinguish it carefully from a similar-sounding formula used elsewhere in the book, warning against treating the two as identical in force. The predicate of the sentence is princes, and the clause that names them is a relative one, more closely defining just which men are meant. It is the kind of grammatical patience that keeps a bare list from being misread, fixing exactly the weight the Chronicler meant his formula to carry.

Sparse as the notice is, the witness makes it serve the chapter’s design. The register is not merely listing names but marking out leaders — men singled out by name because their houses had grown numerous and their standing in the tribe was real. Keil and Delitzsch’s care over the phrase mentioned by their names keeps the reader from passing over it as empty formula: it is the Chronicler’s way of certifying these thirteen as the recognized princes of Simeon, the heads whose fathers’-houses had multiplied enough to furnish an expedition. Jehu and Joel and the rest are enrolled, then, not as bare names but as chiefs of increasing families, named on purpose because it was they who led their people out. The verse yields no story of its own, only names and a pedigree; but the witness makes even that serve, showing how the register quietly ranks its men before it sends them into action.
31:
The first group of Simeon’s towns ends — Bethmarcaboth, Hazarsusim, Bethbirei, and Shaaraim — with the note that these were their cities unto the reign of David, and the witnesses fasten on both the names and that closing clause. The grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott reads the names for their meanings: Bethmarcaboth is house of chariots, Hazarsusim village of horses, while Bethbirei he takes as a corrupt writing of Bethlebaoth, house of lionesses (Josh. 19:6), recalling that there were lions in the wilds of Judah (1 Sam. 17:34), and Shaaraim, two gates, he equates with the Sharuhen known even from the Egyptian inscriptions.

Over the clause unto the reign of David the witnesses offer differing accounts of how Simeon lost these towns. The wesleyan-arminian Adam Clarke holds that David took some of the Simeonite cities and added them to Judah — Ziklag among them (1 Sam. 27:6) — and that, since Simeon had withdrawn its allegiance from the house of David, the kings of Judah pressed their dominion as far as they could into that tribe’s territory. John Wesley, his fellow, lays the loss rather at Simeon’s own door: several of these cities, though given the tribe by Joshua, were never wrested from the Philistines through the tribe’s sloth and cowardice until David’s time, and having so forfeited their right by neglect, the Simeonites saw David take the towns and annex them to his own tribe. Ellicott only asks the question the clause raises — whether it means the cities passed from Simeon in David’s age — and observes that Ziklag, at least, was then a Philistine borough.

So the witnesses weave the verse from geography and history together. The names still breathe of an older, wilder land — chariots and horses, gates and lionesses — while the closing clause marks the moment that land slipped from Simeon’s hold. Whether it was David’s encroachment upon a disloyal tribe, as Clarke thinks, or Simeon’s own forfeit through neglect, as Wesley urges, the upshot is the same: the cities that had been the tribe’s unto the reign of David were the tribe’s no longer after it, and the register, in that single clause, quietly records another stage in Simeon’s long absorption into Judah.
30:
Three more of Simeon’s towns are named — at Bethuel, and at Hormah, and at Ziklag — and here the grammatical-historical Charles Ellicott stands alone over the verse, tracing each name through its variant forms and its later history. Bethuel, he notes, is called Chesil in one passage of Joshua and Bethul in another, a contraction of the same kind as Hamul for Hamuel a few verses back; the town wore more than one form of its name in the parallel lists, and the reader must hold them together as one place, lest a mere difference of spelling be mistaken for a difference of towns.

Hormah he identifies with the ancient Zephath, the Canaanite town that Judah and Simeon devoted to destruction and so renamed (Judg. 1:17), known in his own day, he says, as Sepata. And Ziklag — later so famous — he locates near it, at a site east of Sepata, citing the notices of the town in Joshua (Josh. 15:30-31) and in the history of David, to whom the Philistine Achish would give it for a dwelling (1 Sam. 27:6).

Though a single witness holds the verse, the names carry their own weight, and Ellicott’s patient identifying lets that weight be felt. Hormah is the very place whose name means the ban, a memorial in Simeon’s own territory of a Canaanite city given over to the LORD for destruction — the same fate the tribe would deal to the peoples of Gedor and Seir at the chapter’s end. Ziklag would become the town of David’s exile, the base from which he ruled before he came to the throne; that it lay here among Simeon’s cities binds the lesser tribe’s obscure settlements to the great king’s story. Ellicott does not labour these connections, but he fixes the places precisely, and the register, tracing Bethuel and Hormah and Ziklag, keeps in its dry list of towns the names that Israel’s later history would make its own. A single witness is enough, here, to show that even Simeon’s obscurest holdings were places where the greater story of the nation would one day be enacted — the ban at Hormah, the exile of David at Ziklag — so that the register, in three bare names, touches both the tribe’s ancient wars and its greatest king.
36:
The list of princes runs to its fullest here — Elioenai, and Jaakobah, and Jeshohaiah, and Asaiah, and Adiel, and Jesimiel, and Benaiah — seven names in a single verse, and the witnesses handle them as part of the one company. The grammatical-historical Keil and Delitzsch enumerate them among the leaders whom the following summary will call princes in their families, whose fathers’-houses had increased to a multitude; these seven, with the men named before and the long pedigree of Ziza that the register appends after them, traced through five generations, make up the thirteen chiefs of the Hezekian expedition.

Charles Ellicott, the other grammatical-historical voice, pauses on one of the seven. Jaakobah, he explains, means literally to Jacob — a patronymic formed from the name of the patriarch, much as the English surname Jacobs is formed from Jacob. It is a small philological note, but a fitting one, for it fastens even this obscure Simeonite prince, by the very shape of his name, back to the father of all the tribes.

The witnesses thus read the crowded verse as the heart of the muster-roll. Seven heads of families are named together, otherwise unknown to Scripture, yet set down here because they belonged to the recognized princes of Simeon in Hezekiah’s day. Keil and Delitzsch keep them within the sweep of the summary that certifies them as chiefs of multiplying houses; Ellicott, catching in Jaakobah the echo of Jacob, quietly reminds the reader whose sons these are. Between the two, the verse does its work: it enrolls the leaders of the tribe’s great venture, men remembered for a single enterprise, and binds them — by descent, by name, and by their place in the register — to the ancient stock of Israel from which, however small their tribe had grown, they still sprang. Seven names in a line, with nothing told of any one of them beyond his rank: it is the plainest kind of genealogy, and yet the witnesses will not let it pass as mere filler. Keil and Delitzsch weigh its place in the summary, Ellicott the meaning of a single name, and between them they show that even here, at the crowded centre of the list, the Chronicler is doing deliberate work — enrolling the recognized heads of a multiplying tribe, and reaching, through Jaakobah’s very name, back to Jacob himself.
